children.title: Developmental changes in response to endothelins and receptor subtypes of isolated rat duodenum
children.metadata: Kaoru Irie; Yoko Uchida; Emiko Fujii; Takamura Muraki; Pharmacology; Tokyo Women's Medical College; Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo; Research Publication; Academic Manuscript
children.content: Kaoru Irie *, Yoko Uchida, Emiko Fujii, Takamura Muraki<|txt_split|>Department of Pharmacology, Tokyo Women's Medical College, 8-1 Kawada-cho, Shinjuku-ku, Tokyo 162, Japan<|txt_split|>Received 12 October 1994; revised 29 November 1994; accepted 2 December 1994

children.title: Abstract
children.metadata: Endothelin; Endothelin receptor subtype; IRL 1620; FR19317; RES-701-1; Duodenum; Rat; Contractile response; Biphasic response; Sustained contraction; MAPK cascade; ET4 receptor antagonist; Endotoxin effect
children.content: The response of isolated duodenum to endothelin-1, -3 and IRL 1620 (Suc1Glu\(_4\)A\(^{15-17}\)) endothelin-1 (8-21), a selective endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{4}}\) receptor agonist, was studied in both neonatal (1-week-old) and adult rats by recording mechanical activity ionetically. Endothelin-1, -3 and IRL 1620 (1-100 nM) elicited sustained contraction of neonatal duodenum, in a dose-dependent manner, compared with control. The response of endothelin-1 to endothelin-1 or endothelin-1 and -3 (10-1000 nM) of adult duodenum was biphasic, i.e., transient relaxation followed by contraction, with a potency order of endothelin-1 > endothelin-3. The contractile response to endothelin-1 of adult but not neonatal duodenum was significantly antagonized by pretreatment with FR19317 (1 μM), an endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{4}}\) receptor antagonist. An endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{4}}\) receptor antagonist was administered intraperitoneally into the duodenum at a dose of 1000 nM for 2 h prior to endotoxin injection. In contrast, the response of RES-701:1 (1 μM) did not affect the response to endothelin-1 of either adult or neonatal duodenum. These results indicate that the duodenal response to endothelins changes from a sustained contraction in neonates to a biphasic response in adults. The contractile response to endothelial duodenum is suggested to be mediated through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{4}}\) receptors or via the MAPK cascade. The effect of this receptor antagonist on endothelin-induced duodenum through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{4}}\) receptors. The mechanism of the endothelin-induced response of duodenum was also studied.<|txt_split|>Keywords: Endothelin; Endothelin receptor subtype; IRL 1620; FR139317; RES-701-1; Duodenum; (Rat)

children.title: 1. Introduction
children.metadata: Endothelin isolation; Hypertension induction; Receptor distribution; Biphasic response; Gastric fundus contraction; Duodenal neuropeptide response; TRH-induced contraction; ATP-induced contraction; Nicotine-induced relaxation; Neuropeptide Y transcription; Endothelin-1 developmental change; ET_A receptor mediation; ET_B receptor involvement; Transient relaxation phase
children.content: Endothelin was originally isolated from porcine endothelial cells as a peptide to induce hypertension and the expression of which is regulated by various receptors are distributed in various organs, including the gastrointestinal tract (Matsumoto et al., 1989; Takahashi et al., 1990; Arai et al., 1990; Sakurai et al., 1990, 1991). Endothelin-I induces a biphasic response, with a maximum effect on the cardiovascular system, in guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1990, 1992; Mastroi and Paiva, 1990, 1992). The response of the longitudinal muscle of the rat gastric fundus is reduced after intravenous administration alone (Fulginiti et al., 1991; Shimomura et al., 1994).<|txt_split|>We have studied developmental changes in the response of rat duodenum to various neuropeptides and biologically active substances such as thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH),neuropeptide Y,ATP and \(\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}\) receptors (Bakker et al., 1989;Iri et al.,1993,1994).The duodenal response to TRH,ATP and nicotine was contraction in the neonatal period, but relaxation in the adult Tonoue et al., 19791981 Furukawa and Nomoto,1989Iri et al., 1994). The response of duodenum to neuropeptide Y receptor agonist is mediated by the activation of the transcription during development (Iri et al., 1993). In this paper, we investigated the response to endothelin-1 and -3 and IRL 1620, a selective endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) receptor agonist (Takai et al., 1992),by isolated duodenum from neonatal and adult rats, and studied the effect of the drug on the expression of endothelin-1. We found that the response of rat duodenum to endothelin-1 changes from an \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\)<|txt_contd|>receptor-mediated contraction in neonates to an \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) (10) and the \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) (11) contraction preceded by transient relaxation in adults.

children.title: 2.1. Preparation and measurement of mechanical activity of duodenum
children.metadata: Male Wistar-Irnamichi rats; neonatal and adult rats; duodenum isolation; organ bath setup; modified Locke solution; isotonic transducer; KCl-induced contraction; endothelin response; statistical analysis; Student's t-test
children.content: Male Wistar-Irnamichi rats aged 1 and 9-15 weeks were used. Neonatal rats were killed by decapitation and adult rats stunted by a blow to the head and bled. The duodenum of each animal was removed, and the spleen was removed from the liver, spleen, and bulb (1.5 cm) of neonatal rats and \(2.2\mathrm{cm}\) for adult rats), was suspended in an organ bath containing \(5\mathrm{ml}\) of modified Locke solution (composition in mM: NaCl 154, KCl 402, \(\mathrm{CaCl}_2\) 1.36, \(\mathrm{MgCl_2}\) 0.9, \(\mathrm{NaHCO}_3\) 2.97, glucose 5.56, and sodium 1.8). The sedation was maintained at \(37^{\circ}C\) with continuous bubbling of air. The mechanical activity of the segments under a load of \(0.5\mathrm{g}\) (neonate) and \(1\mathrm{g}\) (adult) was recorded with an isotonic transducer (Nihonkohden TD-1125) on a polyacrylamide gel gels. The amount of sedation required for equilibration, the duodenum was exposed to \(50~\mathrm{mM}\) KCl (hypertonic supply) for \(2\mathrm{min}\) repeatedly (2-3<|txt_contd|>times) and then the response to endothelin was examined. The data are expressed as percentage of the 50 mM KCl-induced contraction (tonic phase). Student's \(t\) -test was used to assess the significance of the difference between group means, and \(P <   0.05\) was considered significant.

children.title: 2.2.Drugs
children.metadata: endothelin-1; endothelin-3; IRL 1620; N7-nitro-L-arginine methyl ester; apamin; RES-701; Res-701-1; dimethyl sulfoxide; physiological saline; acetic acid
children.content: The following drugs were used: endothelin-1, endothelin-3 and IRL 1620 (Suc-[Glu]Ara1.05) endothelin-1 (8-21) (Peptide Institute, Osaka, Japan), N7-nitro-L-arginine methyl ester and apamin (Sigma), 2-(N-methyl-2-hydroxyphenyl)-2-(N-methyl-2-azepinyl)carbonyl[amino]-4-methyl[pentanoyl] amino-3-[3-(1-methyl-1H-iodo]propionyl]amino-3-(2-pyridyl)- propionic acid) kindly provided by Fujisawa Pharmaceutical Co., Osaka, Japan), RES-701 (acyl [Gly]-Asp[3-[1-ethyl-1H-iodo]pyrpropanyl]amino-3-(2-pyridyl)- propionic acid) kindly provided by Fujisawa Pharmaceutical Co., Osaka, Japan). Res-701 (acyl [Gly]-Asp-Tras-His-Gly-Thra-Ala-Pro-Asp-Trp-Phe-Ala-Pro) was dissolved in \(0.7\%\) acetic acid to make \(100\mu \mathrm{M}\) and diluted to adequate concentration with physiological saline. Res-701-1 was dissolved in dimethyl sulfoxide and a 5-fold dilution of the solution was added, which, Other drugs were dissolved in physiological saline, which was added to the bath as a 30- or \(100\mu \mathrm{l}\) aliquot.

children.title: 3.1. Response of neonatal duodenum to endothelium and IRL 1620
children.metadata: Endothelin-1 concentration-dependent contraction; neonatal duodenum response; tachyphylaxis to endothelins; endothelin-3 comparable to endothelin-1; IRL 1620 dose-response profile; pD₂ values comparison
children.content: Endothelin-1 elicited contraction of neonatal duodenum in a concentration-dependent manner (Figs. 1A and 2A). A lower concentration of endothelin-1 induced an increase in spontaneous activity and a slowly increasing response to endotoxin-1 was observed in rats in \(3 - 5\mathrm{min}\) Fig.1Aa).A higher concentration of endothelin-1 elicited a steep contraction (Fig. 1Ac). Since repeated exposure to endothelins led to tachyphylaxis (Fig. 1Ac), second response to endothelin-1 was \(64\pm 4\%\) \((n = 6)\) of first response), each preparation was used for only one series of cumulative concentrations of endothelin-1, and the maximal concentration was \(50~\mathrm{mM}\) KCl-induced contraction. The response of neonatal duodenum to endothelin-3 was not signifi<|txt_contd|>cantly different from that to endothelin-1 (Fig. 2A). The response to IRL 1620 was smaller than that to endothelin-1 in lower concentrations \((1 - 10\mu \mathrm{M})\) but of similar magnitude at \(30 - 100\mathrm{nM}\) Fig.2A).The \(\mathsf{pD}_2\) values were significantly higher from concentration-response curves, for endothelin-1, -3 and IRL 1620 were 8.70, 8.77 and 8.42 respectively.

children.title: 3.2 Response of adult duodenum to endothelins
children.metadata: Endothelin-1 biphasic response; duodenal contraction dynamics; tachyphylaxis to endothelin; H2O2/CO2-induced contraction; endothelin-3 response; CCK comparison; relaxation-contraction profile
children.content: Endothelin-1 elicited a biphasic response in adult duodenum, i.e., transient relaxation followed by sustained contraction, although the early relaxation phase was not observed with a lower concentration of endothelin-1 (Fig. 1B). The contractile phase induced by endotoxylates and/or \(\beta\)-blockers was also associated with phasic and tonic contractions (Figs. 1B(c) and 4B). A strong tachyphylaxis was produced by a repeated exposure to endothelins (Fig. 1B(d)). The contraction due<|txt_contd|>to the second exposure to endothelin-1 was \(26 \pm 5\%\) (Fig. 1B). The median time of response to the second exposure \((n = 4)\) remained at a relevant response (Fig. 1B0C).<|txt_split|>We tested only one concentration of endothelin in the presence of \(10\%\) \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(10\%\) \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) for \(30\mathrm{min}\) to determine if \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2\) or \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) induced contraction as a reference. The re<|txt_contd|>sponse of adult duodenum to endothelin-3 was also biphasic, but significantly smaller than that elicited by the same dose of \(10\%\) of CCK. The response was small transient relaxation without contraction of adult duodenum (data not shown). The negative logarithms of the concentrations which produced \(50\%\) of the KCl:KOH ratio were 7.87 and 6.78 for endothelin-3 and -3 respectively.

subnode.title: 3.2 Response of adult duodenum to endothelins_1
subnode.metadata: Biphasic response; Transient relaxation; Sustained contraction; Tachyphylaxis; Endothelin-1 exposure; Contractile phase; Phasic contraction; Tonic contraction; Reduced early relaxation; Repeated exposure; Diminished response
subnode.content: Endothelin-1 elicited a biphasic response in adult duodenum, i.e., transient relaxation followed by sustained contraction, although the early relaxation phase was not observed with a lower concentration of endothelin-1 (Fig. 1B). The contractile phase induced by endotoxylates and/or \(\beta\)-blockers was also associated with phasic and tonic contractions (Figs. 1B(c) and 4B). A strong tachyphylaxis was produced by a repeated exposure to endothelins (Fig. 1B(d)). The contraction dueto the second exposure to endothelin-1 was \(26 \pm 5\%\) (Fig. 1B). The median time of response to the second exposure \((n = 4)\) remained at a relevant response (Fig. 1B0C).

subnode.title: 3.2 Response of adult duodenum to endothelins_2
subnode.metadata: endothelin-3; biphasic response; H2O2; CO2; CCK; KCl:KOH ratio; duodenum contraction; transient relaxation
subnode.content: We tested only one concentration of endothelin in the presence of \(10\%\) \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2\) and \(10\%\) \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) for \(30\mathrm{min}\) to determine if \(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}_2\) or \(\mathrm{CO}_{2}\) induced contraction as a reference. The response of adult duodenum to endothelin-3 was also biphasic, but significantly smaller than that elicited by the same dose of \(10\%\) of CCK. The response was small transient relaxation without contraction of adult duodenum (data not shown). The negative logarithms of the concentrations which produced \(50\%\) of the KCl:KOH ratio were 7.87 and 6.78 for endothelin-3 and -3 respectively.

children.content: The effects of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) and \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) receptor antagonists were examined on the endothelin-1-induced response of duodenum from both neonatal and adult rats. The response of neonatal duodenum to endothelin-1 (Fig. 3a) was significantly inhibited by FR33917 \((1\mu \mathrm{M}, 15\mathrm{min})\), an endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) antagonist (Sogabe et al., 1993) (Fig. 3A). In adult rat duodenum, pretreatment with FR33917 \((1\mu \mathrm{M}, 15\mathrm{min})\) significantly antagonized the contractile response of duodenum to endothelin-1 (Figs. 3B and 4). FR33917, however, did not affect the response of duodenum during the contraction of duodenum by endothelin-1 (Fig. 4A(a)). The phasic contraction in response to a higher concentration \((1\mu \mathrm{M})\) of endothelin-1 was rather resistant to FR33917 (Fig. 4B(b)). The relaxation induced by endothelin-1 was smaller (but not statistically significant) in the FR33917, and RES-801-1-potassium secretion than in the control duodenum (Figs. 3B and 5B).<|txt_split|>The duodenal response to endothelin-1 was not significantly antagonized by RES-701-1 \((10\mu \mathrm{M}, 15\) and \(20\mu \mathrm{M})\) but was significantly antagonized by RES-701-2 \((3\mu \mathrm{M})\) (Fig. 1), in either neonatal or adult duodenum (Fig. 5). RES-701-1 \((3\mu \mathrm{M})\) however, significantly antagonized the contractile response to lower \((1\) and \(10\mu \mathrm{M})\) but not to higher \((20\mu \mathrm{M})\) levels of endothelin-1 in the duodenum (Fig. 5A). This concentration \((3\mu \mathrm{M})\) of<|txt_contd|>RES-701-1 was sufficient to abolish the endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) receptor-mediated relaxant response to \(0.1\mu \mathrm{M}\) endothelin-3 of rat thoracic aorta precontracted by \(0.1\mu \mathrm{M}\) norepinephrine (data not shown).

Graph A shows that in neonatal preparations, FR139017 (100 nM) significantly reduces ET-1-induced contraction compared to saline control, while vehicle (saline) has no effect. children.title: 3.4. Mechanism of duodenal response to endothelin-1
children.metadata: duodenal response to endothelin-1; tetrodotoxin inhibition; nitric oxide role; apatin block; calcium-activated K+ channel; contractile vs. relaxant response
children.content: The mechanism of duodenal response to endothelin-1 was examined by using several drugs. Tetrodotoxin (TTO) was used in the duodenum and colon, neonatal or adult duodenum to endothelin-1 (Fig. 6), whereas the concentration of tetrodotoxin used was sufficient to inhibit the duodenal response induced by TTO. The effect of TTO on the intestinal and adult rats (Tonoue et al., 1979; Irie et al., 1994).<|txt_split|>Since endogenous nitric oxide (NO) was reported to be involved in endothelin-induced vasodilation, an NO synthase inhibitor was examined to determine a possible role of NO in the relaxant response of adult rat duodenum to endothelin-1. Neither the relaxant nor the contractile response of duodenum to endothelin-1 was inhibited by pretreatment with \(N^{\mathrm{D}}\)-nitro-\(t_{2}\)-nitro-\(t_{3}\)-nitro-\(t_{4}\)-nitro-\(t_{5}\)-nitro-\(t_{6}\)-nitro-\(t_{7}\)-nitro-\(t_{8}\)-nitro-\(t_{9}\)-M, 15 min, Fig.6B). This concentration of \(N^{\mathrm{D}}\)-nitro-\(t_{4}\)-arginine significantly inhibited the relaxant response of adult duodenum to nicotine (Irie et al., 1991).<|txt_split|>Apatin \((0.5\mu \mathrm{M},15\mathrm{min})\) a \(\mathrm{Ca^{2 + }}\) -activated K channel blocker (Hugues et al., 1982), significantly blocked the endothelin-1-induced relaxation but not the contraction of adult duodenum (Fig. 6B). A cyclohexane-treated adult duodenum \((0.5\mu \mathrm{M},30\mathrm{min})\) did not inhibit the contractile response of adult duodenum to endothelin-1 (data not shown).

Tetrodotoxin, \(N^{\mathrm{G}}\)-nitro-\(t\)-arginine methyl ester and aguanin were added to the bath \(15\mathrm{min}\) prior to endothelin-1. Number of preparations in A was 4 for each group. \(^{*}P < 0.05\) vs. endothelin-1-induced relaxation in saline control by Student's t-test.
children.metadata: Tetrodotoxin, endothelin-1, nNOS, L-NAME, agonist, adult, neonatal, Student’s t-test
children.content: The image displays two bar graphs (A and B) illustrating the effect of tetrodotoxin (1 µM) on endothelin-1 (ET-1)-induced relaxation in neonatal (A) and adult (B) duodenal smooth muscle. Graph A shows that in neonatal duodenal tissue, ET-1 (1 nM to 100 nM) induces relaxation, which is significantly reduced by tetrodotoxin, with L-NAME (nNOS inhibitor) and apamin (protein kinase C inhibitor) showing additive effects. Graph B, for adult tissue, shows similar trends but with a lower magnitude of ET-1-induced relaxation and greater inhibition by tetrodotoxin. Both graphs indicate that the ET-1-induced relaxation is significantly reduced by tetrodotoxin, with L-NAME and apamin adding to the inhibition. The results are presented as mean ± SEM with n = 4 per group, and statistical significance (p < 0.05) is indicated by asterisks. The bar graphs are labeled with the respective inhibitors and concentrations.

children.title: 4. Discussion
children.metadata: endothelin-1; endothelin-3; ET_A receptors; ET_B receptors; duodenal response; developmental changes; Ca²⁺ sensitivity; cholinergic innervation; tachyphylaxis; receptor subclassification; neonatal vs. adult; relaxant vs. contractile; NO pathway; K⁺ channels; prostaglandin role; endothelin receptor subtypes
children.content: Immunoreactive endothelin-1 and -3 were found in the plasma of rats with a mean serum concentration of 0.25 (Matsumoto et al., 1989; Takahashi et al., 1990). Mes<|txt_contd|>senger RNA for endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) and \(\mathrm{ET_B}\) receptors was reported to be expressed in the rat and bovine gastrointestinal tract (Arai et al., 1990; Sakurai et al., 1990). Therefore, endogenous endothelins may play an important physiological role in gastrointestinal activities. The human endothelin receptor subtypes are characterized by \(\mathrm{ET_{B}}\) and \(\mathrm{ET_{P}}\). The former is selectively activated by endothelin-1 rather than by endothelin-3, and the latter is equally activated by endothelin-1 and endothelin-3 (Arai et al., 1990; Sakurai et al., 1990). Recently, a subclassification of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{A}}\) and \(\mathrm{ET_B}\) receptors has been proposed, based on their differential sensitivity to endothelin receptor antagonists (Sudjarwo et al., 2000; Sutcliffe et al., 2000). In addition, endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_{A}}\) receptors may be subclassified into RES-701-1-sensitive and -resistant \(\mathrm{ET_{B}}\) receptor subtypes.<|txt_split|>The response of adult rat duodenum to endothelin-1 was biphasic, i.e., transient relaxation followed by contraction, consistent with previous reports on guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1990; Miasiro and Pava, 1990). The response to the drug was more rapid in the acute phase. Thus we add a new example of developmental changes of duodenal responses to biologically active substances. To explain why these substances contract duodenum in neonatal rats but relax it in adult rats, we speculated that the predominant innervation in rat duodenum changes developmentally from excitatory to inhibitory processes. In addition, we hypothesize that cholinergic nerves (Irie et al., 1994). The contraction of duodenum in response to endothelins was greater than that elicited by high \(\mathbf{K}^+\) depolarization in neonates. The increased sensitivity of neonatal duodenum to endothelins is possibly due to endothelin-induced increases in the \(\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}\) sensitivity of contractile elements, which are thought to be related to the activation of the Ca\(_2\)chymo induce \(\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}\) sensitivity in smooth muscle (Karaki, 1989; Somliyo and Himpens, 1989; Hori et al., 1993).<|txt_split|>Our finding that the potency of endothelin-3 for inducing contraction of neonatal duodenum was equal to that of endothelin-1 suggests that endothelin-induced contraction of neonatal duodenum is mediated by a direct effect on the endogenous \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) receptor agonist, IRL 1620, also induced contraction of neonatal duodenum. However, RES-701-1 only weakly antagonized the IRL 1620-induced contraction and failed to antagonize the endothelin-1-induced contraction. This finding may be due to the fact that the concentration of RES-701-1, because this concentration of RES-701-1 antagonized the endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) receptor-mediated relaxation of rat thoracic aorta elicited by endothelin-3 in our study (not shown). Also, \(5\mu \mathrm{M}\) RES-701-1 is reported to antagonize the endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{B}}\) receptor-mediated relaxation of rat thoracic aorta in rats in COS-7 cells (Tanaka et al., 1994). Therefore, the endothelin-induced contraction of neonatal duodenum<|txt_contd|>may act through RES-701-1-resistant endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_B}\) receptor subtypes.<|txt_split|>The relative potency of endothelins for eliciting contraction of adult rat duodenum was endothelin-1 > endothelin-3, and the tonic phase of the endothelin-1-induced contraction of adult duodenum was significantly antagonized by pretreatment with FR139317. These results indicate that the tonic phase of endothelin-1 is not affected by the contraction of duodenum through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptors. As for FR139317 it was ineffective when it was added to the bath during the contraction of duodenum by endothelin-1, the binding of endothelin-1 to the endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptor seems to be very strong, which explains the strong tachyphylaxies produced by endothelins, especially those involved in the contractile response of adult duodenum. Since the effect of the agonist ACTH on endothelin-1 was rather resistant to FR139317, phasic and tonic contractile responses may be mediated by different subclasses of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptors.<|txt_split|>The potency order (endothelin-1 > endothelin-3 > IRL 1620) for endothelin-induced relaxation suggests the mediation of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_2\) receptors. However, the relaxation was antagonized by both FR139317 and RES-701-1. Further studies are required to resolve this discrepancy in the relaxant response of rat adult duodenum.<|txt_split|>These results indicate that the endothelin receptor subtype mediating contractile response changes from a single subunit to a complex of receptors. The development of developmental change of endothelin receptor subtypes in duodenum is not known. It should be pointed out that Eguchi et al. (1994) have shown that the endothelin receptor subtype of vascular smooth muscle cells, namely, the \( \mathrm{Ca^{2+}} \) receptor, is also expressed in the contractile (i.e., differentiated) stage \( (\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}) \). The change in endothelin receptor subtype could be confirmed by receptor binding studies and by investigating human RNA for endothelin receptors in duodenum.<|txt_split|>The duodenal response to endothelin-1 was not affected by pretreatment with tetrodotoxin in either neonatal or adult rats, consistent with the intestinal response to endothelin-1 in guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1998; Mastro and Palva, 1990), indicating that the effect of the endotoxin-induced relaxation on endothelin-1-induced relaxation by apamin suggests the involvement of the \(\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}\)-activated \(\mathbf{K}^+\) channel in the relaxant response to endothelins, as observed in guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1992). Since an inhibitor for NO synthase had no effect on the endothelin-1-induced relaxation of duodenum, the intestinal response to endothelin-1-induced relaxation is dependent on the NO pathway. Although prostaglandin is reported to be involved in the endothelin-induced contraction of rat gastric fundus (Shimomura et al., 1994),<|txt_contd|>prostaglandin plays no role in the duodenal contraction and the development of the mucosal barrier with the case of guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1990).<|txt_split|>In conclusion, the present study shows that endothelin induces sustained contraction in neonatal duodenum, whereas it elicits a transient relaxation followed by contraction in adult rat duodenum, and that it is also induced by the endotoxin duodenum through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) receptors in the neonate but through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) receptors in the adult.

subnode.metadata: endothelin-1; ET_B receptor; FR139317; duodenum contraction; ET_alpha receptor; tachyphylaxis; ACTH resistance; phasic vs tonic response
subnode.content: may act through RES-701-1-resistant endothelin \(\mathrm{ET_B}\) receptor subtypes.The relative potency of endothelins for eliciting contraction of adult rat duodenum was endothelin-1 > endothelin-3, and the tonic phase of the endothelin-1-induced contraction of adult duodenum was significantly antagonized by pretreatment with FR139317. These results indicate that the tonic phase of endothelin-1 is not affected by the contraction of duodenum through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptors. As for FR139317 it was ineffective when it was added to the bath during the contraction of duodenum by endothelin-1, the binding of endothelin-1 to the endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptor seems to be very strong, which explains the strong tachyphylaxies produced by endothelins, especially those involved in the contractile response of adult duodenum. Since the effect of the agonist ACTH on endothelin-1 was rather resistant to FR139317, phasic and tonic contractile responses may be mediated by different subclasses of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\alpha}\) receptors.

subnode.title: 4. Discussion_5
subnode.metadata: endothelin receptor subtype; endothelin-induced relaxation; FR139317 antagonism; RES-701-1 antagonism; developmental change; ET₂ receptor mediation; Ca²⁺ receptor expression; receptor binding studies; human RNA analysis
subnode.content: The potency order (endothelin-1 > endothelin-3 > IRL 1620) for endothelin-induced relaxation suggests the mediation of endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_2\) receptors. However, the relaxation was antagonized by both FR139317 and RES-701-1. Further studies are required to resolve this discrepancy in the relaxant response of rat adult duodenum.These results indicate that the endothelin receptor subtype mediating contractile response changes from a single subunit to a complex of receptors. The development of developmental change of endothelin receptor subtypes in duodenum is not known. It should be pointed out that Eguchi et al. (1994) have shown that the endothelin receptor subtype of vascular smooth muscle cells, namely, the \( \mathrm{Ca^{2+}} \) receptor, is also expressed in the contractile (i.e., differentiated) stage \( (\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}) \). The change in endothelin receptor subtype could be confirmed by receptor binding studies and by investigating human RNA for endothelin receptors in duodenum.

subnode.title: 4. Discussion_6
subnode.metadata: endothelin-1; tetrodotoxin; apamin; Ca²⁺-activated K⁺ channel; NO pathway; prostaglandin; intestinal response; guinea-pig ileum
subnode.content: The duodenal response to endothelin-1 was not affected by pretreatment with tetrodotoxin in either neonatal or adult rats, consistent with the intestinal response to endothelin-1 in guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1998; Mastro and Palva, 1990), indicating that the effect of the endotoxin-induced relaxation on endothelin-1-induced relaxation by apamin suggests the involvement of the \(\mathrm{Ca^{2+}}\)-activated \(\mathbf{K}^+\) channel in the relaxant response to endothelins, as observed in guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1992). Since an inhibitor for NO synthase had no effect on the endothelin-1-induced relaxation of duodenum, the intestinal response to endothelin-1-induced relaxation is dependent on the NO pathway. Although prostaglandin is reported to be involved in the endothelin-induced contraction of rat gastric fundus (Shimomura et al., 1994),

subnode.title: 4. Discussion_7
subnode.metadata: prostaglandin; duodenal contraction; mucosal barrier; endothelin; neonatal duodenum; adult rat duodenum; endotoxin; ET_A receptors
subnode.content: prostaglandin plays no role in the duodenal contraction and the development of the mucosal barrier with the case of guinea-pig ileum (Lin and Lee, 1990).In conclusion, the present study shows that endothelin induces sustained contraction in neonatal duodenum, whereas it elicits a transient relaxation followed by contraction in adult rat duodenum, and that it is also induced by the endotoxin duodenum through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) receptors in the neonate but through endothelin \(\mathrm{ET}_{\mathrm{A}}\) receptors in the adult.
